turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Breyer has our opinion this morning in case 17-9560, Rehaif v. United States.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: There is a federal statute that makes it unlawful for people who fall into certain categories to posses firearms. Under the categories best known is probably felons. If you are a convicted felon, you cannot possess a firearm, but the one at issue here is the category of aliens who are in the country unlawfully. They cannot possess a firearm or it says it is unlawful. Then a different statute says it imposes criminal penalties on anyone who 'knowingly violates' the first statute. And the question at issue here is that word knowingly. Does it apply just to the possession of firearm, the conduct, or does it also apply to alien unlawfully in the United States, the status of the person? And between those two, we choose the second. We think that it applies to both and you have to both know to be convicted, the government has to show both that he knowingly possessed the firearm and also that he knowingly knew he was an alien unlawfully in the United States. We have two basic reasons. The first reason is we think the language is somewhat better read that way. It says knowingly and knowingly then has a bunch of elements. The elements are two, I just mentioned about four times, knowing you are an unlawful alien and knowing that you possessed a firearm and knowingly linguistically would not seem to skip around and pick out some elements and not others. But the main reason is I think the second, which is our interpretation is consistent with a very fundamental principle that underlies the criminal law. It is at least a presumption and a strong one that you have to show the government and convict somebody of a crime, to show that you have to show what Blackstone called a vicious will, and that means we have a presumption that Congress intended scienter, knowledge or some kind of state of mind that was a bad state of mind, that presumption dates back to the foundation of our system of criminal justice, it is found throughout the common law. It reflects the view that individuals should be sent to prison only if they have knowledge of the facts that made their conduct wrongful. Now, here that defendant's status often separates that which is wrongful from that which is not. There are quite a few people who own guns perfectly lawfully, and the difference as to whether it is a crime for them to own the gun or possess it is what their status is, are they an unlawful alien or not. So I mean, imagine a child, a small child unlawfully brought into the United States and many years later he possesses a gun, he might not know that he was unlawfully brought into the United States, and that which would distinguish the wrongful behavior from the non-wrongful behavior is the knowledge that he is there unlawfully. Now, for those reasons and others reasons that we set forth in our opinion, we reverse the decision below and hold as I said that knowingly applies to both. Justice Alito has filed a dissenting opinion, which Justice Thomas joins.
